narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 55.3% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use for approval. This prediction is based on your profile: you're a female student from a middle-income family, planning to attend full-time, with a White racial background, a solid LSAT score of 39, an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, and notably low performance in your school's deciles for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades—ranking in the 2nd decile compared to typical students who average around the 5th or 6th. While your LSAT stands out as stronger than the average of 36.7, your undergrad GPA is a bit below the typical 3.21, and your early and later school deciles are well below the dataset averages of about 5.7 and 5.5. In assessing your case, the model weighed several factors, with your LSAT score having the strongest influence, unexpectedly nudging the prediction toward a higher risk of failure despite its above-average strength. Next in influence were your low Year 1 decile rank, followed closely by your low Year 3 decile, both contributing toward the failure prediction by signaling weaker academic performance relative to peers. Your middle family income level came after that, also pushing in the same direction, then your undergrad GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status, each playing smaller but still failure-leaning roles in the overall decision. Compared to typical students in our dataset—who often have higher school deciles, slightly better GPAs, and more varied backgrounds—your lower grade rankings stood out as bigger drags, even as your LSAT provided some counterbalance that wasn't enough to tip the scales. This ranking shows how the model prioritizes test scores and consistent academic deciles most heavily for bar success predictions. Keep in mind, this is just one data-driven estimate, not a final judgment on your potential. To boost your future chances, focus on realistic steps like intensive bar exam preparation courses, which can directly address performance gaps shown in your school deciles and GPA—many students overcome similar profiles through dedicated study. You might also consider retaking the LSAT if opportunities arise, given its top influence here, or building stronger study habits early in law school to improve those decile-like metrics. With effort, you can shift the odds in your favor and pursue your goals.
